rulings.Case Title: JOHN GIDDING et al VS. IVAN KAYE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion To Hold Judgment Debtor In Contempt For Failure To Comply With Court'S Order Compelling Responses To Discovery & For Issuance Of Body Attachment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 31, 2013, Line 1: Defendant GLENDONBROOK WINES PTY LTD'S (Amended Notice Of Motion And) Motion To Hold Judgment Debtor In Contempt For Failure To Comply With Court's Order Compelling Responses To Discovery & For Issuance Of Body Attachment Pro Tem Judge Nils Rosenquest, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The Court is concerned and would appreciate discussion and/or authority on the following issues: (1) As a discovery sanction, contempt is available for failure to appear at a deposition or under a subpoena, but contempt is not specifically authorized in the discovery statutes as a sanction for failure to respond to interrogatories or produce documents. See CCP §§ 2030.300; 2031.320 [§§ 708.020 and 708.030 incorporate the sanction provisions of the general discovery statutes]. Does this omission rule out contempt proceedings for failure to make written post-judgment discovery? (2) Contempt is a drastic remedy and authorized only where the court's integrity or dignity are at issue and is rarely, if ever, appropriate for discovery sanctions. See In re de la Parra (1986) 184 Cal.App.3d 139. It does not appear that the court's integrity is impugned by the judgment debtor's failure to respond to post judgment discovery, and other post judgment remedies appear available, such as an order of examination, or further proceedings in Luxembourg. Why shouldn't the court issue an Order of Examination (upon appropriate application) to be served under the Hague Convention or by personal delivery (see below)? Is there reciprocity with Luxembourg that would entitle the judgment creditor to enforce proceedings in Luxembourg? (3) Assuming that a general contempt proceeding under CCP §§ 1209 et seq. is appropriate or available, shouldn't the court issue an OSC re: contempt pursuant to CCP §1212 first? It does not appear that there are sufficient facts to sustain a warrant of attachment in the first instance, because the judgment debtor never appeared or contested the original motion to compel. It also does not appear the court can assume willful non-compliance because intent is an element of the contempt offense to be proved at trial. (4) Finally, since general contempt is a quasi-criminal proceeding, it does not appear that the court has jurisdiction absent service of the papers on the judgment debtor in the same manner as a summons. This implicates CCP 413.10(c), because the judgment debtor is out of the country. If Luxembourg is a signatory to the Hague Convention, is service under the Hague Convention the exclusive means of service? Is it possible under California law to serve the papers (OSC or OEX) personally in Luxembourg? =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN H.A. DOE VS. LUIS ARMANDO VILLA GONZALEZ et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn To Exclude Pltff'S Testimony As Evidentiary Sanction For Failure To Appear At Noticed Deposition
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 31, 2013, Line 4: Defendant DIGNITY HEALTH, INC.'S Motion To Exclude Plaintiff's Testimony As Evidentiary Sanction For Failure To Appear At Noticed Deposition Pro Tem Judge Nils Rosenquest, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted with sanction as follows: Court notes that no opposition has been filed, but also that a testimonial sanction in the first instance is drastic. Accordingly, the Court believes it appropriate to issue an alternative order compelling plaintiff's attendance at a deposition on a date selected by moving party no later than November 12, 2013, or, if plaintiff fails to appear, granting the testimonial sanction. Moving party may submit a conforming order at the hearing or, if the plaintiff does not give required notice to appear and argue, by email: nrosenquest@earthlink.net. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to nrosenquest@earthlink.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARK EMMETT WILSON VS. MIGUEL ALEJANDRO RESCALVO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn For Sanctions Regarding X-Complainants
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 31, 2013, Line 9, CROSS DEFENDANT MARK WILSON's Motion For Sanctions Regarding Cross-Complainants. The motion is denied. Plaintiff fails to show that the cross-complaint is frivolous within the meaning of CCP 128.7. The request to strike references to Plaintiff's spouse is denied; further, it is unclear what information by page and line is sought to be stricken. The Court denies defendant's request for sanctions. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: DAWN-SHEMAIN WEEKS et al VS. CMEA DEVELOPMENT COMPANY, LLC et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Discovery
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 31, 2013, Line 6: Plaintiffs DAWN-SHEMAIN WEEKS', MARGARET HINES', and SHANNON SCHLAGENHAUF'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Discovery Pro Tem Judge Nils Rosenquest, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Prior to the hearing, the parties are to meet and confer concerning the terms of a suitable protective order pertaining to the requested documents.At the hearing, the Court requests that the parties (a) address the privacy issues with particularity, identifying the precise privacy interests of CMEA, Haag, and Handelsman and the countervailing necessity by plaintiffs; and (b) why a protective order prohibiting disclosure outside of the litigation would not sufficiently protect those privacy interests. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LOANVEST XII L.P. VS. THE BUILDER INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition For Compelling Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 31, 2013, Line 15, DEFENDANTs THE BUILDER INC., CHARLES BEARD and RONALD STICKNEY's Petition For Compelling Arbitration. The Court grants the motion because the plaintiff does not oppose arbitration and a stay. The Court denies the moving party's request for attorney's fees. The moving party failed to demonstrate that it made a request for arbitration and that plaintiff rejected that request. A demand for arbitration is a necessary prerequisite under CCP 1281.2. Fees are not recoverable because this motion may have been averted if moving party made the required arbitration demand. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)
